replies.title: "The truest things are said in jest"
replies.content:
This is an odd phenomenon that occurs when the formal side of things is pretty much on target, but there is no intentional forceful use of "will". A casual, light-hearted act or utterance can produce an unexpected, more or less on-the-nose, effect.

Of course, this is pretty hard to replicate, since one of the base conditions seems to be the "unintentional" nature of the act. But it's good to be aware of the possibility, if only to avoid getting a result one might regret!

This is somewhat related to what a friend of mine used to refer to as the "word of truth" effect. My friend's idea was that constantly (faithfully) speaking the truth creates a kind of momentum behind the speaker's words. Someone who is accustomed to speak the truth at all times will sometimes find that some statement not intended too seriously will turn out to have been exactly true. This phenomenon, used sparingly, is a little more able to be used intentionally, though trying to exploit it seems to quickly undermine its effectiveness.

LeGrand

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Excellent! Yes, and I recite it daily as part of my morning worship of the gods. It's Hymn 72, to the Genius. Here's the version (lightly revised from the Thomas Taylor translation) that I use:

Thee, mighty-ruling, Genius dread, I call,
mild Jove, life-giving, and the source of all:
Great Jove, much-wand'ring, terrible and strong,
to whom revenge and tortures dire belong.
Mankind from thee, in plenteous wealth abound,
when in their dwellings joyful thou art found;
Or pass thro' life afflicted and distress'd,
the needful means of bliss by thee supprest.
'Tis thine alone endued with boundless might,
to keep the keys of sorrow and delight.
O holy, blessed spirit, hear my prayer,
disperse the seeds of life-consuming care;
With fav'ring mind the sacred rites attend,
and grant a happy life, a blessed end.

The guardian genius is the Pagan version of the holy guardian angel. As the hymn shows, it was understood in ancient times primarily as the dispenser of what we call karma and they called fate, but I've found that regular use of this hymn helps open channels of communication with the guardian genius.

comments.title: Geomantic Temples
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I've been working on the practices in your geomancy book. When I started my meditations on the figures, I began to envision imaginal temples for each one. For example, the temple to Albus takes the form of a giant goblet-like tower made of white stone. The "goblet" is filled with water, and this placid pool ripples intensely when disturbed in any way. At the middle of the pool is a platform housing the temple's vestibule, which for Albus is a space for contemplation. One can sit here and endlessly ponder the rippling of the pool. Getting to and from the vestibule is difficult and it feels tempting to just stay there forever.

The pool contained within a tower expresses the elemental correspondences (water within air), the shape of the tower resembles the figure, the rippling water suggests mental activity and the isolated vestibule conveys the insular and unassertive character of Albus. It's the same basic idea as the imaginal temples of the spheres that you describe in Revisioning the Tree of Life. I didn't base my envisioning of the temples on that book, though, this activity is something that just sprang into my mind as I began the series of geomantic meditations. I wanted to know, in your study of the Renaissance literature on geomancy did you come across any work describing imaginal locations for the figures? If there are original sources on such things I'd like to find out how close my imaginings are to others.

Perhaps at some point I can create renditions of these temples as I imagine them. I'm not the best at drawing or sculpting though and I don't want to use AI for something like this.

Also, I have another question. I've been working on this at the same time as some GD study using other books of yours; I'm still in the early stages. replies.title: Re: Geomantic Temples
replies.content:
1) No, I haven't encountered anything of the kind in the older literature. That said, imaginal temples of this sort can be extremely useful in many kinds of occult work. I'm very glad to hear that you're developing a set for the geomantic figures and encourage you to find some way to make them publicly available when you're satisfied with them -- having multiple people working with them will put power into the egregor.

2) Geomancy was a key Golden Dawn practice, one of the few modes of practical work introduced in the Outer Order and developed further in the Inner Order. Thus you can combine geomantic meditations and divinations with GD work with no trouble -- and indeed with excellent results.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Temples

comments.title: Crossing the Abyss!
comments.content:
Hi JMG, hope you're doing well. Thanks for another MM!

What is your view on the operation of "Crossing the Abyss?" I find it funny that after getting in touch with your Higher Self (or Holy Guardian Angel in thelemic parlance) you still "cross the abyss" to "destroy your ego." But destroy the ego if you have contact with your Higher Self and got in touch with your individuality? Or is the angel a separate entity?

Thanks a lot!

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content:
My views here are very idiosyncratic; they're derived from material I learned from John Gilbert, and he cheerfully admitted that most occultists disagreed with him. (He got the material from his teachers, who apparently had the same attitude.) The only justification I can offer for my view is that it works better than the alternative.

The view in question? Human souls can't cross the Abyss. That's simply the way things are. The three highest spheres of the Tree, the primal triad in John's terminology, belong wholly to the Divine; the seven lower spheres are the realm of created beings; the Abyss is the boundary between them, and it is the Ring-Pass-Not for us, and for all created entities.

As for "destroying the ego," I wrote about that here:

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-no-ego-ego-trip-an-interlude/

The short form is that everyone I've ever met who claimed to have destroyed or transcended their ego behaved like a raging egomaniac. Thus I don't think they actually did away with their egos -- I think they just repressed their egos, and the return of the repressed had its usual effect. The genuinely enlightened people I've met have egos; their secret is that they don't take their egos seriously. They treat their egos with the kind of amused compassion you'd direct at an unusually clumsy puppy. They're also much better company, and much wiser teachers, than the self-important types who go on and on about how they've gotten rid of their egos.

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content: Not the OP, but that does seem much more realistic as to what happens with the ego “after” enlightenment. My question is what is the phenomenon of ego death? People like Eckhart Tolle who say they have experienced this - what really happened there and is it the goal?

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content: I don't think "ego death" is actually the death of the ego. I think it's a hide-and-seek game the ego plays with itself. As I noted above, I think they just repressed their own awareness of their egos, which is why they tend to be such egotists!

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content:
Here are some speculations about a material phenomenon frequently associated with introspective descriptions of "enlightenment" or "ego death", and about that phenomenon's relation to its supernatural correlates. (It might seem as though I didn't put much effort into those speculations, and that's half-true, but I think the speculations are substantially higher-grade than you might expect just given that fact.)

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/359082.html?thread=59230890#cmt59271082

My impression is that the threshold at which people start using the phrase "ego death" is when a certain semi-illusion's defense processes (or the most-subjectively-"visible" layer of its defense processes, at least) are temporarily overcome for any significant length of time, whereas Zen practitioners like to reserve the term "enlightenment" for situations where the semi-illusion is replaced by a different (and less illusion-prone) configuration of motives-towards-cognitions in a way that's more sustainable and is on track to refine itself over time.

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content:
Ah, that's very interesting. I recognize "ring pass not" from Fortune's Cosmic Doctrine, it's about time I take a look at that book again.

If I makey ask another one: what do you think happens to those who TRY to Cross the Abyss? What do you make of this Choronzon figure?

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content: The ego is really good at working up grand melodramas in which it can be a persecuted victim, a conquering hero, or both at once. The grand battle with Choronzon is a great example. I'm sure quite a few people don't realize that they're shadowboxing with a projection, and take to the melodrama with utter seriousness, but I've yet to meet any such people who strike me as genuinely enlightened, or even moving in that direction -- au contraire, one of the embarrassing things about Western occultism in recent years is how many of its big names don't even seem to have achieved ordinary maturity. That's par for the course for a spiritual tradition that draws most of its members from the fringes of society, but there it is.

replies.title: Re: Crossing the Abyss!
replies.content: Got it! Loved your article about the ego, by the way. Many thanks!!

replies.title: (no subject)

The two systems don't relate to each other and it's a mistake to make them try to do so. comments.title: Yeat's Wheel
comments.content:
At one point, if I remember correctly, you wrote that the final leap off the Wheel can happen at any point on the second half of the Wheel.

How can that be when there are phases when unity of being breaks apart? In the phase after yours, The Assertive Man, the soul "desires to be strong and stable, but as Unity of Being and self-knowledge are both gone, and it is too soon to grasp at another unity through primary mind, he passes from emphasis to emphasis." I don't see how that person can unite with his Higher Self, or a deity.

So were you oversimplifying when you wrote "second half of the wheel?"

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: Yeat's Wheel
replies.content: Nope. When Yeats talks about Unity of Being he's referencing the expression of that unity in the four Faculties -- Will, Creative Mind, Mask, and Body of Fate. Those are the temporary expression of four eternal Principles -- Husk, Spirit, Passionate Body, and Celestial Body -- which always possess Unity of Being. If the person of Phase 19, in passing from emphasis to emphasis, reaches one that allows Will to attune itself fully to Husk and Creative Mind to do the same thing to Spirit, even briefly, that's the final incarnation for that soul, and at death -- when the Faculties dissolve back into the Principles -- the transition to the Thirteenth Cone takes place. The much rarer experience of leaving the wheel while incarnate (the sort of thing that produces genuine living saints and sages) is mostly limited to the late 4th quarter, but the more common experience of making contact in life and then achieving liberation at death can happen anywhere.

replies.title: Re: Clarification on cleansing

comments.title: Piscean apocalypse
comments.content:
I have been contemplating something you said in some blog post or podcast, about how the Piscean age is coming to an end about this year, and how the transition to the Aquarian age could be bumpy. And I see the Piscean dispensations all heading towards a truly apocalyptic showdown in the land of their birth. I see these religions of peace whipping everyone into war, the religions of mercy leading to horrid cruelty and madness. Where does it end? Many say it will cause the end of the world, which only fuels the Piscean apocalyptic madness. I don't believe in the linear narrative of time of the Piscean religions, and believe the world and life cannot end, just change. But still, nuclear war is a terrifying prospect.

Perhaps it was this fear that led to the dream I had, but I have asked the Gods and the infinite consciousness of the self-aware universe to give me a clue as to how this Piscean age ends. I will recount the dream I had last night and leave you all to discuss what it may mean.

Terrible war rages across the desert. Cities burn, soldiers die, the rivers run red with blood, and the only remaining structures are towers of bodies that reach for the sky. The Jewish star, Christian Cross, and Muslim crescent are all stained red with blood. Everyone is willing to fight and kill and die for their holy book. No one will budge an inch. Nuclear armageddon is threatened, first by Isreal, then Iran has them. Flaming mushrooms threaten to bloom.

And then... then Jerusalem goes up in flames. It's the only nuke that goes off. It wasn't even set off by the fanatics. A consortium of concerned scientists, military and government officials, mostly from nations that don't believe in Abrahamic faiths, like India or China, came together to try and save the world from its faiths. They decided that if the children couldn't play with this toy, they would take it away. They apologize for the deaths but declare that its a far, far lower body count than full nuclear war would have produced, and thus was the lesser of the two evils. The leaders of this group reveal themselves, that they acted independently of any government (a lie, India and China supported their efforts to save the world) and that they themselves had perished in the blast, so there's no one to hate, no one to avenge, no one to fight.

The faithful of all 3 Abrahmic faiths are faced with a blow to the foundations of their faiths. You can no longer believe in a physical literalist version of these faiths. They all involved an end of the world in Jerusalem, specifically regarding certain events on the temple mount. This cannot happen now. Jerusalem is gone forever. You can't even visit the site without dying. The temple mount, epicenter of the explosion, is literally obliterated. As all these blind faithful realize that their chosen holy person isn't literally physically coming back as they believed, they realize their warring was for nothing. And all the faithful lay down their arms and weed for their dead religions.

Oh, there are still Jews and Christians and Muslims, but they have to interpret their scriptures symbolically, metaphorically and metaphysically. The wide-eyed fundamentalism cannot be sustained. Plus, most of the hardcore faithful have killed each other by this point. Millions leave the Abrahamic faiths. Some to the religion of progress, but after atheist materialists blow up Jerusalem their religion isn't exactly popular either. They drift, to a thousand different faiths, and that is how the Piscean phrophetic faiths fail, how the Piscean age yields to the Aquarian.

I really hope this isn't a prohphetic dream about the end of the prophetic religions. Then again... it's not the worst future we could be facing. It's food for thought, and I look forward to any and all thoughts and comments.

replies.title: Re: Piscean apocalypse
replies.content:
As near as I can tell, the Aquarian age began in 1879; that was certainly the opinion of a lot of occultists back then, and their logic seems sound. Of course that was just the beginning of a long transitional process, which will not finish for centuries.

As for your apocalypse, well, we'll see. Belief in apocalypses is very much of a Piscean habit.

replies.content:

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: Piscean apocalypse
replies.content: Granted, but it still doesn't seem likely to me -- nor, to be frank, do I buy the claim that Abrahamic religions will collapse promptly as a result. Jerusalem has had its entire population slaughtered more than once in its history, and they've just kept on coming back for more.

replies.title: Re: Piscean apocalypse
replies.content:
I wouldn't expect the Abrahamic religions to collapse either. They would assign the label of Jerusalem in the prophecies to another city, and keep on making End Times predictions, while other sects of those religions ignore those predictions or make different ones. The OP's scenario would cause a crisis of faith among some Jews, Christians, and Muslims and surely some of them would seek other religions. The OP would be mistaking the scale and the permanance of that crisis, but he wouldn't be entirely wrong about the second part if the first part happens. It probably won't.

However, I am worried about nukes being used, because Israel makes threats about the Samson Option and might use it (or just launch nukes on the cities of enemy states, if the government retains some sanity) if it takes too much damage from Iran in this war. And if so, Jerusalem could get nuked if Iran had secretly made a few nukes.

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: Piscean apocalypse
replies.content: It wouldn't surprise me at all if a few mushroom clouds were involved in the next set of major Middle Eastern wars. The thing is, nuking Jerusalem wouldn't remove it from play. When the Romans conquered Jerusalem after the revolt of Simon bar Kokhba they leveled the city to the ground, built a new Roman city there, imported a population from elsewhere, and forbade Jews to live there under penalty of death. When the Crusaders took Jerusalem they slaughtered every man, woman, and child in the city -- contemporary records claim that they rode through blood more than a foot deep in the streets on their way to pray at the shrine of the Prince of Peace. Neither of those events changed the city's status. If it got nuked -- well, Hiroshima and Nagasaki are both thriving cities now, you know...

comments.title: Above and Below
comments.content:
I’m stuck between focusing on spirit - that transcendent source with, in and through all things - and accepting my limited physical earthly existence.

How are the two integrated?

When we focus on spirit, are we neglecting or avoiding our earthly life? A lot of folks would say finding the spirit is the only thing that matters.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Above and Below
replies.content: One of the basic lessons of occult philosophy is that whenever you have a rigid duality like that, you're missing the third factor that unites them. Between matter and spirit is life, which partakes of both. The notion that we all ought to ignore the material world and focus entirely on spirit, like the opposite but equally silly notion that we ought to ignore the spiritual world and focus entirely on matter, is a little like insisting that human beings all ought to be chopped in half at the waist. We live in all three worlds and our task is to bring them all into harmony!

comments.title: Neutralizing to manifest
comments.content:
I was wondering if I might implore you to elaborate on something you mentioned in your analysis of The Cosmic Doctrine: The Law of Polarity. You wrote, "If the lower self systematically neutralizes all its characteristic activities on all the planes on which it is normally active, the Higher Self will sometimes manifest. This is the key to certain kinds of religious and mystical practice"

About manifesting the Higher Self: I apologize if this is a silly or naive question, but what makes this a goal worth pursuing? We are already emanations of our Higher Selves, no? How is it different from turning one's will against oneself, which is a big spiritual no-no?

And relatedly, I'd like to know about what "kinds of religious and mystical practice" you had mind? I suppose this is a reiteration of my first point as the practices likely shed light on their reasons/purposes...but I figured I'd ask.



I'll also add on a tangentially related report:
The Hanged Man card has been making a pronounced appearance in my readings as of late so I've been focusing my meditations on it's imagery/symbolism. When I took an insight I gleaned from my musings to the Ogham Fews I carved into clay (for divination purposes), Gort landed in front of me multiple times in a row. The themes of the Card and the Few do overlap, and yet I was still shocked when I discovered that they also happen to share the 12th position in their relative sets. This isn't the first time I've had a Few reading match a Tarot one, but I'm not sure I'll ever get used to how strange and almost spooky these kinds of synchronicities feel.

replies.title: Re: Neutralizing to manifest
replies.content: I share a similar feeling about the Higher Self. Limited contact for the purposes of accomplishing some important aim, sure. But permanent union with it? Nah, I'm nowhere near ready for that. Ask me again in a few eons when my Lower Self has grown into my Higher Self organically.

replies.title: Re: Neutralizing to manifest
replies.content:
The process as described in the Cos.Doc. is intended to bring about temporary manifestations of the Higher Self for the sake of spiritual guidance, or to deal with some problem that the lower self has tried and failed to master. It's not meant to bring about a permanent state -- you'd have to turn your lower self into a complete zombie to make that happen, and your Higher Self would simply slap you silly and tell you to get on with life.

The spiritual and mystical practices in question are certain kinds of meditation and contemplative prayer, together with austerities, meant to bring the activities of the lower self to a complete standstill. Most of the mystics talk about that.

replies.title: Re: Neutralizing to manifest
replies.content: I've sometimes wondered if the true defining trait of the Axial Age religions isn't the promise that you can end all your problems forever by turning yourself into some kind of zombie or other, and playing word games to try to make this sound palatable: "It's OK, it's not your true self!"

replies.title: Re: Neutralizing to manifest
replies.content: I'd see this not as a defining trait but a common pathology. The defining trait is the promise that there's a perfect world (or not-world) waiting for you, if only you accept a specific ideology and the practices that come with it.

replies.title: Re: Consecrated Statues
replies.content:
1) The method as described in classical sources is very easy, because it depends on natural magic. To make an agalma -- that's the Greek word for an empowered statue -- you simply get a hollow image, and then fill it with substances that correspond with the influence you're trying to attract. For example, if you wanted to invoke Venus in this way, you'd get a hollow statue of the goddess and then fill it with stones, herbs, and other substances that all correspond to Venus. Then you start making daily offerings and prayers to the goddess, using the statue as a vehicle for her presence. It seems to work quite well, and the effect builds powerfully over time.

2) Very glad to hear this!

comments.title: On the Higher Self
comments.content:
On the subject of the Higher Self, I read a story several years ago that has a scene that I suspect captures some aspect of our relationship with our Higher Self: at several points in the story, the protagonist encounters a being of light who aids her or pushes her on.

At the very end she meets directly with the being, who is impelling her further on, and the protagonist asks if she has a choice in the matter. The being tells her: "You have a choice, but you've already made it," and she suddenly realizes that the being is her future self, after she has transcended ordinary time and space. It knows what choice she will make because it remembers making it.

This is sort of how I think of our Higher Self as both something always already present since the beginning of our existence and as something we have to become in our own time.

comments.title: Planetary Correspondences and the Great Old Ones

replies.title: Re: Planetary Correspondences and the Great Old Ones
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. In fact, I assigned all the Great Old Ones who were central to my tentacle novels to the Tree of Life:

[IMAGE:0]

Those correspondences can be challenged, of course. If I had to rework them on a purely planetary basis, I'd have Shub-Ne'Hurrath as the Moon, the primary feminine power; Nyarlathotep as Mercury; Yhoundeh the elk goddess as Venus; Hastur, the King in Yellow and monarch of the Great Old Ones, as the Sun; Ithaqua as Mars; Azathoth as Jupiter; Tsathoggua as Saturn; Yog-Sothoth as Uranus; and Cthulhu as Neptune.
